title: Bobbieן¿½s Statue
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grown tired of mourning for my sins,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And brooding over merits,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The other night with aching heart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I went amongst the spirits;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I met one that I knew well:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O Scottyן¿½s Ghost! is that you?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And did you see the fearsome crowd
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At Bobbie Burnsן¿½s statue?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They hurried up in hansom cabs,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tall-hatted and frock-coated;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They trained it in from all the towns,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The weird and hairy-throated;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They spoke in some outlandish tongue,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They cut some comic capers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ilka man was wild to get
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His name in all the papers.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They showed no sign of intellect,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Those frauds who rushed before us;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They knew one verse of ן¿½Auld Lang Syneן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The first one and the chorus.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They clacked the clack oן¿½ Scotlanן¿½ן¿½s Bard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They glibly talked of ן¿½Rabbyן¿½;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But what if he had come to them
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Without a groat and shabby?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They drank and wept for Rabbieן¿½s sake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They stood and brayed like asses
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (The living bardן¿½s a drunken rake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dead one loved the lasses);
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If Bobbie Burns were here, theyן¿½d sit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As still as any mouse is;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If Bobbie Bums should come their way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½d turn him out their houses.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O weep for bonny Scotlandן¿½s Bard!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And praise the Scottish nation,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who made him spy and let him die
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heart-broken in privation:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Exciseman, so that he might live
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through northern wintersן¿½ rigours,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just as in southern lands they give
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hard-up rhymer figures.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We need some songs of stinging fun
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To wake the States and light ן¿½em;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I wish a man like Robert Burns
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were here to-day to write ן¿½em!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But still the mockery shall survive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till Day oן¿½ Judgement crashes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The men we scorn when weן¿½re alive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With praise insult our ashes.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Scottyן¿½s Ghost said: ן¿½Never mind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The fleas that you inherit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The living bard can flick ן¿½em off,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They cannot hurt his spirit.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The crawlers round the poetן¿½s name
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall crawl through all the ages;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His workן¿½s the living thing, and they
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are fly-dirt on the pages.ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
